name: Burrito.Callback

values.name: andApply
values.comment: Shortcut for `andThen << apply`
values.type: t -> Burrito.Update.Update a msg t -> Burrito.Update.Update a msg t

values.name: apply
values.comment: Append a callback to the list of functions that subsequently get applied to the returned value using `runCallbacks`.
values.type: t -> a -> Burrito.Update.Update a msg t

values.name: runCallbacks
values.comment: Sequentially compose the list of monadic functions (callbacks) produced by a nested update call.
values.type: Burrito.Update.Update a msg (a -> Burrito.Update.Update a msg t) -> Burrito.Update.Update a msg t

name: Burrito.Update

aliases.name: Update
aliases.comment: Type wrapper for Elm's `( model, Cmd msg )` tuple.

values.name: addCmd
values.comment:
Add a command to an `Update` pipeline. For example;

    update msg state =
        case msg of
            SomeMsg someMsg ->
                state
                    |> addCmd someCommand
                    |> andThen (addCmd someOtherCommand)
                    |> andThen (setStatus Done)

In this example, `andThen (addCmd someOtherCommand)` can also be shortened to
[`andAddCmd`](#andAddCmd)`someOtherCommand`.
values.type: Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg -> a -> Burrito.Update.Update a msg t

values.name: andAddCmd
values.comment: Shortcut for `andThen << addCmd`
values.type: Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg -> Burrito.Update.Update a msg t -> Burrito.Update.Update a msg t

values.name: andMap
values.comment:
Trying to map over a function `number -> number -> number`,

    map (+) (save 4)

we end up with a result of type `Update (number -> number) msg t`. To apply the function inside this value to another `Update number msg t` value, we can write…

    map (+) (save 4) |> andMap (save 5)

in `elm repl`, we can verify that the result is what we expect:

    > (map (+) (save 4) |> andMap (save 5)) == save 9
    True : Bool

This pattern scales in a nice way to functions of any number of arguments:

    let
        f x y z =
            x + y + z
    in
    map f (save 1)
        |> andMap (save 1)
        |> andMap (save 1)

If not sooner, you'll need this when you want to `mapN` and N > 7.

See also [`map2`](#map2), [`map3`](#map3), etc.
values.type: Burrito.Update.Update a msg t -> Burrito.Update.Update (a -> b) msg t -> Burrito.Update.Update b msg t

values.name: andThen
values.comment:
Sequential composition of updates. This function is especially useful in combination
with the forward pipe operator (`|>`), for writing code in the style of pipelines. To chain
updates, we compose functions of the form `something -> State -> Update State msg t`:

    say : String -> State -> Update State msg t
    say what state = ...

    save state
        |> andThen (say "hello")
        |> andThen doSomethingElse

_Aside:_ `andThen` is like the monadic bind `(>>=)` operator in Haskell, but with the arguments interchanged.
values.type: (b -> Burrito.Update.Update a msg t) -> Burrito.Update.Update b msg t -> Burrito.Update.Update a msg t

values.name: ap
values.comment: This function is like [`andMap`](#andMap), but with the arguments interchanged.
values.type: Burrito.Update.Update (a -> b) msg t -> Burrito.Update.Update a msg t -> Burrito.Update.Update b msg t

values.name: join
values.comment:
Remove one level of monadic structure. It may suffice to know that some other
functions in this library are implemented in terms of `join`. In particular, `andThen f = join << map f`
values.type: Burrito.Update.Update (Burrito.Update.Update a msg t) msg t -> Burrito.Update.Update a msg t

values.name: kleisli
values.comment:
Right-to-left (Kleisli) composition of two functions that return `Update` values,
passing the state part of the first return value to the second function.
values.type: (b -> Burrito.Update.Update d msg t) -> (a -> Burrito.Update.Update b msg t) -> a -> Burrito.Update.Update d msg t

values.name: map
values.comment: Apply a function to the state portion of a value.
values.type: (a -> b) -> Burrito.Update.Update a msg t -> Burrito.Update.Update b msg t

values.name: map2
values.comment:
Apply a function of two arguments to the state portion of a value.
Equivalently, we can think of this as taking a function `a -> b -> c` and
transforming it into a “lifted” function of type `Update a msg t -> Update b msg t -> Update c msg t`.
values.type: (p -> q -> r) -> Burrito.Update.Update p msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update q msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update r msg t1

values.name: map3
values.comment: Apply a function of three arguments to the state portion of a value.
values.type: (p -> q -> r -> s) -> Burrito.Update.Update p msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update q msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update r msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update s msg t1

values.name: map4
values.comment: Apply a function of four arguments to the state portion of a value.
values.type: (p -> q -> r -> s -> t) -> Burrito.Update.Update p msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update q msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update r msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update s msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update t msg t1

values.name: map5
values.comment: Apply a function of five arguments to the state portion of a value.
values.type: (p -> q -> r -> s -> t -> u) -> Burrito.Update.Update p msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update q msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update r msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update s msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update t msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update u msg t1

values.name: map6
values.comment: Apply a function of six arguments to the state portion of a value.
values.type: (p -> q -> r -> s -> t -> u -> v) -> Burrito.Update.Update p msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update q msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update r msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update s msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update t msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update u msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update v msg t1

values.name: map7
values.comment: Apply a function of seven arguments to the state portion of a value.
values.type: (p -> q -> r -> s -> t -> u -> v -> w) -> Burrito.Update.Update p msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update q msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update r msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update s msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update t msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update u msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update v msg t1 -> Burrito.Update.Update w msg t1

values.name: mapCmd
values.comment: Map over the `Cmd` contained in the provided `Update`.
values.type: (msg -> msg1) -> Burrito.Update.Update a msg t -> Burrito.Update.Update a msg1 t

values.name: run
values.comment: Translate a function that returns an `Update` into one that returns a plain `( model, cmd )` pair.
values.type: (p -> Burrito.Update.Update a msg t) -> p -> ( a, Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg )

values.name: run2
values.comment: Same as [`run`](#run), but for functions of two arguments.
values.type: (p -> q -> Burrito.Update.Update a msg t) -> p -> q -> ( a, Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg )

values.name: run3
values.comment: Same as [`run`](#run), but for functions of three arguments.
values.type: (p -> q -> r -> Burrito.Update.Update a msg t) -> p -> q -> r -> ( a, Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg )

values.name: save
values.comment:
Lifts a value into the `Update` context. For example,

    save model

corresponds to `( model, Cmd.none )` in code that doesn't use this library.
values.type: a -> Burrito.Update.Update a msg t

values.name: sequence
values.comment: Take a list of `a -> Update a msg t` values and run them sequentially, in a left-to-right manner.
values.type: List.List (a -> Burrito.Update.Update a msg t) -> a -> Burrito.Update.Update a msg t

values.name: using
with
values.comment:
Combinator useful for pointfree style. For example, to get rid of the lambda in the following code;

    update msg state =
        case msg of
            Click ->
                state
                    |> updateSomething
                    |> andThen (\s -> setCounterValue (s.counter + 1) s)

we can write:

    update msg state =
        case msg of
            Click ->
                state
                    |> updateSomething
                    |> andThen (with .counter (setCounterValue << (+) 1))

values.name: application
values.comment:
Used as a drop-in replacement for `Browser.application`, but instead creates a `Program`
where `init` and `update` are based on the `Update` type of this library.

    init : flags -> Url -> Navigation.Key -> Update model msg

    update : msg -> model -> Update model msg
values.type: { init : flags -> Url.Url -> Browser.Navigation.Key -> Burrito.Update.Update model msg t, onUrlChange : Url.Url -> msg, onUrlRequest : Browser.UrlRequest -> msg, subscriptions : model -> Platform.Sub.Sub msg, update : msg -> model -> Burrito.Update.Update model msg t, view : model -> Browser.Document msg } -> Platform.Program flags model msg

values.name: document
values.comment:
Used as a drop-in replacement for `Browser.document`, but instead creates a `Program`
where `init` and `update` are based on the `Update` type of this library.

    init : flags -> Update model msg

    update : msg -> model -> Update model msg
values.type: { init : flags -> Burrito.Update.Update model msg t, subscriptions : model -> Platform.Sub.Sub msg, update : msg -> model -> Burrito.Update.Update model msg t, view : model -> Browser.Document msg } -> Platform.Program flags model msg
